original_hypothesis.title: Omnichain bridge transaction approvals never expire, allowing delayed execution under changed conditions
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: OmnichainLogic.updateBridgeTransactionApproval() records an approval timestamp, and startBridgeTransaction() only checks that the waiting time has passed; there is no upper bound expiry. If an approval is granted and later the environment changes (routes compromised, destination address rotated, market moves), the previously-approved bridgeRequest can still be executed at any point in the future as long as its hash matches. This increases operational risk and could be exploited if an attacker can cause managers to approve a transaction hash and later trigger execution when it becomes harmful (still on-chain timing, but requires access to onlyManager).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Non-expiring approvals are a known operational hazard for delayed execution systems; the risk is not purely theoretical because cross-chain ops often involve delays and coordination, and old approvals can resurface.
deployment_assumptions: BridgeRequest hash collisions are not feasible; risk is about reuse of the same approved request later
OnlyManager actors might execute stale approvals inadvertently or under partial compromise
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No deployment scripts show omnichain manager wiring; approval/execution flow described in code and Phase A DF-003.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (approvedBridgeTXN[txn]==0 || approvedBridgeTXN[txn]+WAITING_TIME>block.timestamp) revert; // no max validity window

preconditions: A bridge transaction hash has been approved (approvedBridgeTXN[txn] set)
OnlyManager can later call startBridgeTransaction with the same BridgeRequest

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Two-step approval intended as safety delay, but lack of expiry turns it into a perpetual authorization.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Indefinite after the waiting time passes

operational_questions: Do Watchers/ops treat approvals as one-time intents with assumed short validity?
Is there off-chain tracking to invalidate old approvals?
Can approvedBridgeTXN be cleared operationally if not executed (it can be toggled off, but requires action)?
validation_requirements: Review ops workflow for bridge approvals and cancellations
Test that old approvals remain executable weeks later

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain/observe a legitimate approval for a bridgeRequest hash
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait until conditions make it harmful (route compromised, destination mismatch risk, etc.)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute startBridgeTransaction with the same bridgeRequest under manager authority
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Bridge executes using current route configuration

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Unexpected delayed bridging; potential fund movement contrary to current policy

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Approvals with no subsequent execution for long periods
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Bridge executions referencing very old approval timestamps
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Delayed execution shortly after route/destination changes

validation_result.impact_summary: Bridge transaction approvals never expire after the 30-minute waiting period, allowing indefinitely stale approvals to be executed under changed conditions (routes compromised, destination rotated, market moves).
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies OmnichainLogic.sol lines 33-83. Line 78 contains the check with only a minimum waiting time and no maximum. Line 68 (updateBridgeTransactionApproval) sets `approvedBridgeTXN[transactionHash] = block.timestamp` with no associated expiry. Line 85 clears the approval after execution (`approvedBridgeTXN[txn] = 0`), confirming single-use but not time-bounded use.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Line 78 only enforces `approvedBridgeTXN[txn] + BRIDGE_TXN_WAITING_TIME <= block.timestamp` (minimum delay). There is no upper bound check such as `approvedBridgeTXN[txn] + MAX_VALIDITY >= block.timestamp`. An approval from any point in the past remains valid indefinitely after 30 minutes.
Sets the approval timestamp with no associated expiry field. The toggle mechanism allows revocation but requires active management action, which may not happen if the approval is forgotten.

calling_sequence: 1. Manager calls updateBridgeTransactionApproval(txnHash) → sets approvedBridgeTXN[txnHash] = block.timestamp
2. Time passes: 30 minutes, days, weeks, or months
3. Conditions change: route compromised, destination rotated, market moves significantly
5. Check at line 78: approvedBridgeTXN[txn] != 0 ✓ AND approvedBridgeTXN[txn] + 30min <= block.timestamp ✓ (always true after 30 min)
6. Bridge executes using current route configuration with the old approved request
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path from approval to execution has no upper time bound. At OmnichainLogic.sol:78, the condition `approvedBridgeTXN[txn] + BRIDGE_TXN_WAITING_TIME > block.timestamp` is the ONLY time check. Once BRIDGE_TXN_WAITING_TIME (30 minutes) passes, this condition is permanently false (assuming no overflow, which won't happen with reasonable timestamps), meaning the check never blocks execution again. The approval at line 68 records `block.timestamp` but no expiry timestamp. The execution at line 85 clears the approval (single-use) but this doesn't help if execution happens months later under changed conditions.

deployment_assumptions_checked: BridgeRequest hash collisions are not feasible; risk is about reuse of the same approved request later
OnlyManager actors might execute stale approvals inadvertently or under partial compromise
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided show omnichain manager wiring, keeper/manager configuration for bridge approvals, or any operational workflow for bridge transaction approvals. The deploySharedContracts.ts deploys LZHelperSender but does not configure bridge approval workflows.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is realistic for operational risk: (1) A legitimate bridge transaction is approved via updateBridgeTransactionApproval. (2) Due to operational delays, the transaction is not executed promptly. (3) Days or weeks later, conditions change (e.g., destChainAddress is rotated by maintainer via updateChainInfo, but note the old bridgeRequest still has the old receiverAddress which would now NOT match destChainAddress - this provides partial mitigation). However, if conditions change in ways NOT captured by destChainAddress (e.g., route compromise, token depegging, market conditions), the stale approval remains dangerous. The destChainAddress check at line 81-84 does provide some mitigation against destination changes, but does not cover all scenarios (e.g., the same destination but different market conditions, or the route itself being compromised).
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. The missing expiry creates an indefinite window for execution of stale bridge approvals. Mitigating factors: (1) both approval and execution require onlyManager access, (2) destChainAddress validation partially protects against destination changes, (3) the toggle mechanism allows manual revocation. Aggravating factors: (1) cross-chain operations involve high-value asset movements, (2) recovery from incorrect cross-chain transactions is complex, (3) operational teams may not track all outstanding approvals.
recommended_mitigations: Add a MAX_BRIDGE_TXN_VALIDITY constant (e.g., 24 hours) and check: `block.timestamp <= approvedBridgeTXN[txn] + MAX_BRIDGE_TXN_VALIDITY`

Modify the check at line 78 to: `if (approvedBridgeTXN[txn] == 0 || approvedBridgeTXN[txn] + BRIDGE_TXN_WAITING_TIME > block.timestamp || approvedBridgeTXN[txn] + MAX_BRIDGE_TXN_VALIDITY < block.timestamp)`
Implement an event-based monitoring system to alert on approvals that have not been executed within a reasonable timeframe
Consider auto-expiring approvals by adding a cleanup function or checking expiry in startBridgeTransaction
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy OmnichainLogic-derived contract with test parameters. 2. Call updateBridgeTransactionApproval(txnHash) to approve a bridge request. 3. Advance time by 30 minutes + 1 second → verify startBridgeTransaction succeeds. 4. Deploy fresh instance, approve again, advance time by 30 days → verify startBridgeTransaction still succeeds (demonstrating no expiry). 5. Compare with expected behavior where a MAX_VALIDITY would cause revert after e.g., 24 hours.
uncertainties: The destChainAddress check at lines 81-84 provides partial mitigation for destination rotation scenarios - the approved bridgeRequest's receiverAddress must still match the current destChainAddress mapping
Off-chain operational procedures may include tracking and manually revoking stale approvals, which would mitigate the on-chain defect
The actual likelihood of a manager executing a stale approval depends on operational practices not visible in code
additional_testing_needed: Verify that destChainAddress rotation properly blocks old approvals (it should, since the bridgeRequest encodes a specific receiverAddress)
Test interaction between updateBridgeTransactionApproval toggle and long-delayed execution
Assess whether route changes (via SwapAndBridgeHandler) could make an old approval execute on a different/compromised route
